nodes.name: Art Museums of the World
nodes.description: Major art museums and galleries worldwide

items.prompt: Which museum in Paris, housed in a former royal palace with a modern glass pyramid entrance designed by I.M. Pei, is the world's most visited art museum and home to the *Mona Lisa*, *Venus de Milo*, and *Winged Victory of Samothrace*?
items.explanation:
Paris, France; world's largest and most visited art museum with approximately 10 million visitors annually
Housed in the Louvre Palace, a former royal residence dating to the 12th century as a fortress under Philip II
The glass pyramid entrance, designed by Chinese-American architect I.M. Pei, opened in 1989 and was initially controversial
Home to the *Mona Lisa*, *Venus de Milo*, and *Winged Victory of Samothrace* — its three most famous works
Collection spans from antiquity to the mid-19th century, with over 380,000 objects and 35,000 on display
Opened as a public museum in 1793 during the French Revolution, displaying the royal collection
Covers 72,735 square meters (782,910 sq ft) of gallery space — walking every gallery would take several days
A satellite branch, Louvre Abu Dhabi, opened in 2017 on Saadiyat Island in the UAE
items.cardFront: Paris — *Mona Lisa*, glass pyramid by I.M. Pei

items.prompt: Which museum on Fifth Avenue in New York City, founded in 1870, is the largest art museum in the Americas, with a collection of over 1.5 million objects spanning 5,000 years and 17 curatorial departments?
items.explanation:
New York City, USA; founded in 1870, opened in 1872; largest art museum in the Americas
Main building on Fifth Avenue at 82nd Street along Central Park's eastern edge, designed by Richard Morris Hunt
Collection of over 1.5 million objects spanning 5,000 years, divided into 17 curatorial departments
Notable holdings include the Temple of Dendur (an entire Egyptian temple), European paintings, and the American Wing
The Met Cloisters in Fort Tryon Park houses medieval art in a building assembled from parts of five European cloisters
Admission was pay-what-you-wish for decades; fixed-price tickets for non-New Yorkers were introduced in 2018
The rooftop sculpture garden offers views of Central Park and the Manhattan skyline
Fourth-largest museum in the world by floor area, after the Louvre, Hermitage, and National Museum of China
items.cardFront: New York City — largest art museum in the Americas, Fifth Avenue

items.prompt: Which gallery in Florence, begun in 1560 by Giorgio Vasari as offices for Florentine magistrates, houses Botticelli's *Birth of Venus* and *Primavera* and is one of the oldest art museums in the Western world?
items.explanation:
Florence, Italy; one of the oldest and most famous art museums in the world
Built by Giorgio Vasari in 1560 as offices ('uffizi') for Florentine magistrates under Cosimo I de' Medici
The Medici family's art collection was bequeathed to the city of Florence in 1737 by Anna Maria Luisa de' Medici, last of the Medici line
Home to Botticelli's *Birth of Venus* and *Primavera*, along with works by Leonardo, Michelangelo, Raphael, Titian, and Caravaggio
The Vasari Corridor, an elevated passageway above the Ponte Vecchio, connects the Uffizi to the Pitti Palace across the Arno
One of the first European galleries open to visitors by request (from 1765); formally became a public museum after Italian unification
Housed the world's finest collection of Italian Renaissance art
A car bomb attack by the Sicilian Mafia in 1993 killed five people and damaged several paintings
items.cardFront: Florence — Botticelli's *Birth of Venus*, Medici collection

items.prompt: Which museum in Madrid, opened in 1819, holds the world's finest collection of Spanish painting, including major works by Velázquez (*Las Meninas*), Goya (*The Third of May 1808*), and El Greco?
items.explanation:
Madrid, Spain; opened November 19, 1819, in a neoclassical building designed by Juan de Villanueva
Holds the world's richest collection of Spanish art: Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, Zurbarán, Murillo, Ribera
Velázquez's *Las Meninas* (1656) is the museum's most celebrated painting, displayed in its own room
Goya's works span both his elegant court portraits and his harrowing late 'Black Paintings,' originally painted on his dining room walls
Also strong in Flemish and Italian painting: Bosch's *The Garden of Earthly Delights*, Titian, Raphael, Rubens
The building was originally designed as a natural science museum; Ferdinand VII repurposed it as a royal painting gallery
Collection comprises over 8,000 paintings, though only about 1,300 are on display at any time
A major expansion designed by Rafael Moneo, incorporating the former Jerónimos cloister, opened in 2007
items.cardFront: Madrid — Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, Spanish masters

items.prompt: Which museum in St. Petersburg, founded in 1764 when an empress acquired 225 paintings from a Berlin merchant, occupies the former Winter Palace and contains over three million items spanning two million years of culture?
items.explanation:
St. Petersburg, Russia; founded 1764 by Empress Catherine the Great; open to the public since 1852
Began when Catherine acquired 225 paintings from Berlin merchant Johann Ernst Gotzkowsky
Housed across six buildings including the Winter Palace, the former imperial residence of the Russian tsars
Collection of over three million items makes it one of the largest museum collections in the world
European painting holdings include works by Leonardo, Raphael, Rembrandt (over 20 paintings), Matisse, and Picasso
Catherine's collecting was voracious — she bought entire collections wholesale from European aristocrats and dealers
The museum is famous for its cats: dozens of cats live in the basement to control mice, a tradition dating to the 18th century
The name 'hermitage' means a place of solitude — Catherine originally kept her art in private rooms for personal enjoyment
items.cardFront: St. Petersburg — Winter Palace, Catherine the Great's collection

items.prompt: Which museum in Amsterdam, reopened in 2013 after a decade-long renovation, houses Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* in a specially designed gallery and is the Netherlands' national museum of art and history?
items.explanation:
Amsterdam, Netherlands; national museum of art and history, founded in The Hague in 1800, moved to Amsterdam in 1808
The current building, designed by Pierre Cuypers in a Dutch Neo-Renaissance style, opened in 1885
Closed for a major renovation from 2003 to 2013 by Spanish architects Cruz y Ortiz; reopened to critical acclaim
Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* is the centerpiece, displayed in the purpose-built Gallery of Honour
Also holds Vermeer's *The Milkmaid* and *Woman Reading a Letter*, and works by Frans Hals and Jan Steen
The building straddles a public roadway — cyclists pass through a tunnel beneath the museum
Collection includes over one million objects; approximately 8,000 are on display, focused on 1200–2000 AD
'Rijksmuseum' translates to 'State Museum' in Dutch

items.prompt: Which free-admission art museum on Trafalgar Square in London, founded in 1824 with 38 paintings, now holds over 2,300 works dating from the mid-13th century to 1900, including paintings by Van Gogh, Turner, and Constable?
items.explanation:
London, England; founded in 1824 when the British government purchased 38 paintings from the estate of banker John Julius Angerstein
Located on the north side of Trafalgar Square; the building designed by William Wilkins opened in 1838
Collection of over 2,300 paintings spanning the mid-13th century to 1900 — relatively small but exceptionally high quality
Major holdings include works by Van Eyck, Leonardo, Raphael, Titian, Velazquez, Rembrandt, Vermeer, Turner, Constable, Monet, and Van Gogh
Free admission since its founding — intended to be accessible to all social classes, unlike private aristocratic collections
During WWII, paintings were evacuated to slate mines in Wales; one painting was returned each month for a 'Picture of the Month' exhibition to boost public morale
The Sainsbury Wing extension (1991), designed by Robert Venturi, houses the oldest paintings in the collection
Shares the site with the National Portrait Gallery, located just behind it
items.cardFront: London — Trafalgar Square, free admission, 2,300+ paintings

items.prompt: Which New York museum, founded in 1929, holds Van Gogh's *The Starry Night*, Dalí's *The Persistence of Memory*, and Picasso's *Les Demoiselles d'Avignon*, and played a pivotal role in establishing modern art as a legitimate field?
items.explanation:
New York City, USA; founded November 7, 1929, just nine days after the stock market crash that began the Great Depression
Founded by Abby Aldrich Rockefeller, Lillie P. Bliss, and Mary Quinn Sullivan — three prominent art patrons
Collection of over 200,000 works, including painting, sculpture, film, photography, design, and architecture
Holds Van Gogh's *Starry Night*, Dalí's *Persistence of Memory*, Picasso's *Les Demoiselles d'Avignon*, and Warhol's *Campbell's Soup Cans*
The current building on 53rd Street in Midtown Manhattan has been expanded and renovated multiple times, most recently in 2019
First museum to establish curatorial departments for film, photography, and architecture/design
Alfred Barr, its founding director, organized groundbreaking exhibitions that defined the canon of modern art
The sculpture garden, designed by Philip Johnson, is one of the most celebrated outdoor spaces in New York
items.cardFront: New York City — *Starry Night*, *Persistence of Memory*, modern art canon

items.prompt: Which London museum, opened in 2000 in a converted power station designed by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott on the south bank of the Thames, has a vast Turbine Hall used for large-scale commissions and is Britain's national gallery of modern art?
items.explanation:
London, England; opened May 12, 2000, in the former Bankside Power Station on the south bank of the Thames
The power station, designed by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott (who also designed the red telephone box), operated from 1947 to 1981
Converted by Swiss architects Herzog & de Meuron, who kept the industrial character of the building
The Turbine Hall, the former engine room, is used for major annual commissions: Ai Weiwei's *Sunflower Seeds* (2010), Olafur Eliasson's *The Weather Project* (2003)
Free admission to the permanent collection; one of the most visited museums in the world with about 6 million visitors annually
Connected to St Paul's Cathedral across the Thames by the Millennium Bridge (which famously wobbled when first opened)
A 10-story extension, the Switch House (now Blavatnik Building), opened in 2016
Part of the Tate gallery family, which also includes Tate Britain, Tate Liverpool, and Tate St Ives
items.cardFront: London — former Bankside Power Station, Turbine Hall

items.prompt: Which museum exists in two famous buildings: a 1959 spiraling white rotunda in New York designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, and a 1997 titanium-clad structure in Bilbao, Spain, designed by Frank Gehry that revitalized the city's economy?
items.explanation:
Two iconic buildings: New York (opened 1959) and Bilbao, Spain (opened 1997)
The New York Guggenheim on Fifth Avenue was designed by Frank Lloyd Wright; visitors walk a continuous spiral ramp to view art
Wright's building was controversial when it opened — some artists refused to exhibit, saying the curves distracted from the art
The Bilbao Guggenheim, designed by Frank Gehry, is clad in titanium panels that shimmer and change color in the light
Bilbao's opening in 1997 sparked the 'Bilbao effect' — the idea that a single spectacular building can transform a city's economy and reputation
Founded by industrialist and art collector Solomon R. Guggenheim; the foundation was established in 1937
The New York museum focuses on Impressionist, Post-Impressionist, early Modern, and contemporary art; strong holdings in Kandinsky
Additional Guggenheim venues have operated in Venice (Peggy Guggenheim Collection) and other cities
items.cardFront: New York (Wright spiral) and Bilbao (Gehry titanium)

items.prompt: Which museum guarded by two bronze lion statues on Michigan Avenue in Chicago holds Grant Wood's *American Gothic*, Hopper's *Nighthawks*, and Seurat's *A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte*?
items.explanation:
Chicago, USA; founded in 1879 as both a museum and a school (the School of the Art Institute of Chicago)
Two iconic bronze lions by sculptor Edward Kemeys flank the entrance on Michigan Avenue; installed in 1894
Holds three of America's most famous paintings: Grant Wood's *American Gothic*, Hopper's *Nighthawks*, and Seurat's *Grande Jatte*
Also strong in Impressionism: major works by Monet, Renoir, and Caillebotte (*Paris Street; Rainy Day*)
The Modern Wing, designed by Renzo Piano, opened in 2009 and doubled the museum's gallery space
One of the oldest and largest art museums in the United States; collection of approximately 300,000 works
The museum building was originally constructed for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition
Consistently ranked among the best museums in the world by major travel publications

items.prompt: Which Paris museum, opened in 1986 in a former Beaux-Arts railway station built for the 1900 World's Fair, specializes in Impressionist and Post-Impressionist art and bridges the gap between the Louvre and modern art museums?
items.explanation:
Paris, France; opened December 9, 1986, in the former Gare d'Orsay railway station
The Beaux-Arts station was built by architect Victor Laloux for the 1900 Exposition Universelle; it was one of the first electrified urban rail terminals
The station closed in 1939 when its platforms became too short for modern trains; nearly demolished before being repurposed
Specializes in Western art from 1848 to 1914, filling the chronological gap between the Louvre (pre-1848) and the Centre Pompidou (post-1914)
Holds the world's greatest collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist paintings: Monet, Renoir, Degas, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, Seurat
The giant station clock on the museum's upper level is a famous photo spot overlooking the Seine
The transformation from station to museum was championed by President Valéry Giscard d'Estaing in 1978
Receives approximately 3.5 million visitors annually
items.cardFront: Paris — former Gare d'Orsay railway station, Impressionism

items.prompt: Which complex of galleries in the world's smallest independent state, founded by Pope Julius II in the early 16th century, contains the Sistine Chapel ceiling and the *School of Athens* fresco by Raphael?
items.explanation:
Vatican City; founded by Pope Julius II in the early 16th century; one of the oldest museum collections in the world
The Sistine Chapel, with Michelangelo's ceiling (1508–1512) and *The Last Judgment* (1536–1541), is the culmination of the museum route
Raphael's *School of Athens* (1509–1511) is in the Raphael Rooms (Stanze di Raffaello), painted as a commission from Julius II
Collection spans Egyptian antiquities, Etruscan artifacts, Renaissance masterpieces, and modern religious art
The spiral staircase designed by Giuseppe Momo in 1932 is one of the most photographed staircases in the world
Approximately 7 million visitors annually; the museums are among the most visited in the world
The collection includes the Laocoön and His Sons sculpture group, rediscovered in Rome in 1506
The Sistine Chapel serves as the location for the papal conclave, where new popes are elected
items.cardFront: Vatican City — Sistine Chapel, Raphael's *School of Athens*

items.prompt: Which museum complex in Los Angeles, designed by architect Richard Meier and opened in 1997, sits atop a hilltop in the Santa Monica Mountains, cost approximately $1.3 billion to build, and offers free admission funded by an oil fortune trust?
items.explanation:
Los Angeles, USA; opened December 16, 1997, after 13 years of planning and construction
Designed by architect Richard Meier in white travertine and aluminum panels; sits atop a ridge in the Santa Monica Mountains at the Sepulveda Pass
Cost approximately $1.3 billion to build — one of the most expensive museum projects ever
Funded by the J. Paul Getty Trust, endowed by the oil magnate J. Paul Getty; admission is free (parking is charged)
Collection focuses on European paintings, drawings, sculptures, and decorative arts from the Middle Ages to the present, plus photographs
Notable holdings include Van Gogh's *Irises* (1889), acquired in 1990
The campus includes a 134,000-square-foot Central Garden designed by artist Robert Irwin
A separate site, the Getty Villa in Malibu, houses the collection's Greek, Roman, and Etruscan antiquities
items.cardFront: Los Angeles — Richard Meier hilltop campus, free admission

items.prompt: Which London museum, established by an Act of Parliament in 1753 and opened in 1759, holds the Rosetta Stone, the Elgin Marbles from the Parthenon, and a collection of approximately eight million objects spanning two million years of human history?
items.explanation:
London, England; established 1753, opened to the public January 15, 1759 — the world's first national public museum
Collection of approximately eight million objects spanning two million years of human history and culture
The Rosetta Stone (196 BC), key to deciphering Egyptian hieroglyphs, has been in the museum since 1802
The Elgin Marbles (Parthenon Sculptures), removed from Athens by Lord Elgin in the early 1800s, remain a source of ongoing dispute with Greece
The Great Court, redesigned by Norman Foster with a glass-and-steel roof (2000), is the largest covered public square in Europe
Founded on the collection of physician Sir Hans Sloane, who bequeathed 71,000 objects to the nation
Free admission; one of the most visited museums in the world with approximately 6 million visitors annually
Despite the name, the British Museum is a world cultures museum, not specifically focused on British history or art
